ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic porridge class; no specific Bubur Kanji entry exists. FOODON_03311069 (porridge food product) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Bubur Kanji code. A00LC (rice-based porridge) used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Bubur Kanji; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bubur Kanji is one bowl of approximately 250 g, as commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bubur Kanji — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bubur Kanji (food code F05100008). Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution using HPB coconut milk reference entry, as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat for this dish.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — rice porridge / congee
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bubur Kanji in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 78 is taken from the closest analogous food: white rice porridge / congee (GI range 78–90, median 78) as published in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (78 × 24.6) / 100 ≈ 19.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 76 mg/100 g — well below the 400 mg/100 g high-sodium threshold
hypertension.guidance: Bubur Kanji is low in sodium at 76 mg/100 g and is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided no additional salt or high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or fish sauce are added at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~78) due to prolonged cooking of white rice starch; low dietary fibre at 0.51 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bubur Kanji has a high GI of approximately 78 and low fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g, pair with protein (egg, fish) and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 3.2 g/100 g from coconut milk; total fat 4.26 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk in Bubur Kanji contributes approximately 3.2 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is below the 5 g/100 g high-concern threshold but warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; requesting a reduced-coconut-milk preparation and limiting serving size to 200 g will help keep saturated fat intake within recommended daily limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubur Kanji is a traditional Malay-style rice porridge popular in Singapore, particularly consumed at breakfast or as a light snack. It is prepared by simmering white rice in water or a light coconut milk broth with aromatic spices including turmeric, ginger, and lemongrass until the grains break down into a soft, thick, creamy consistency. The dish is characteristically garnished with crispy fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies) and roasted or fried peanuts, which provide contrasting crunch and umami depth. The flavour profile is mild, savoury, and subtly spiced, with the coconut milk lending a gentle richness. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 92 kcal, 3.44 g protein, 4.26 g fat (of which approximately 3.2 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 9.84 g carbohydrate with only 0.51 g dietary fibre. Sodium is low at 76 mg/100 g. The dish has a high glycaemic index (~78) due to the prolonged cooking of white rice starch, making it a concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is halal-certified, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory phytochemicals (curcumin and gingerols). Bubur Kanji is a culturally significant comfort food in the Malay community and is commonly served during Ramadan as a pre-dawn or post-iftar meal.
common_user_queries: Is bubur kanji suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in bubur kanji?
What is the GI of bubur kanji?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05100008) with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input. GI estimated from the closest analogous food (white rice porridge) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution using HPB reference data. All sources are approved primary sources.
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Foodo source record for F05100008 reports iron as -1.0 (data not available), indicating that iron content for this traditional preparation was not measured or reported in the HPB database as of the record creation date. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this dish is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are not available from other approved sources for this specific preparation. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: no trans fats are expected in this traditionally prepared dish. saturated_fat_g of 3.2 g/100 g is estimated from the coconut milk content of the dish using HPB coconut milk reference data, as the HPB record does not separately itemise saturated fat for this entry. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on white rice porridge (congee) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Bubur Kanji exists in approved sources. The input record described the dish as 'plant_based' in dietary_tags; this has been corrected to non-vegan and non-vegetarian as the dish contains ikan bilis (fish). The 'plant_based' tag has been removed from dietary flags accordingly.
